subject: everyday-science
description: Natural Hazards Disasters Energy Resources MCQs for PPSC, FPSC, NTS, CSS & PMS.

mcqs.q: What is the primary cause of earthquakes?
options: Tectonic plates getting stuck and straining the ground
mcqs.explanation: Earthquakes are caused by tectonic plates getting stuck and putting strain on the ground.

mcqs.q: An earthquake is best described as:
options: A series of seismic waves or tremors in the Earth's crust
mcqs.explanation: An earthquake is a series of seismic waves or tremors in the Earth's crust.

mcqs.q: Plate tectonics is the theory that:
options: The atmosphere is divided into plates
The Earth's outer shell is divided into several plates that glide over the mantle
The Sun is divided into plates
mcqs.explanation: Plate tectonics is the theory that Earth's outer shell is divided into several plates that glide over the mantle.

mcqs.q: Smaller earthquakes can be caused by all of the following EXCEPT:
mcqs.explanation: Smaller earthquakes can be caused by volcanic activity, landslides, mine blasts and nuclear experiments — but not by lunar tides.

mcqs.q: Hot liquid and semi-liquid rock located under Earth's surface is called:
mcqs.explanation: Magma is the extremely hot liquid and semi-liquid rock located under Earth's surface.

mcqs.q: Earthquakes and volcanoes occur mostly in:
mcqs.explanation: Earthquakes and volcanoes occur mostly in folded and faulted regions of the Earth's crust.

mcqs.q: Which instrument is used to detect and record earthquakes?
mcqs.explanation: A seismograph or seismometer is the instrument used to detect and record earthquakes.

mcqs.q: The Richter scale measures the:
mcqs.explanation: The Richter scale (Richter magnitude scale) is a measure of the strength of earthquakes.

mcqs.q: Earthquakes rarely occur in:
mcqs.explanation: Earthquakes rarely occur in Brazil — it sits in the relatively stable interior of the South American Plate.

mcqs.q: The Ring of Fire is also called:
mcqs.explanation: The Ring of Fire is also referred to as the Circum-Pacific Belt — a path along the Pacific Ocean with active volcanoes and frequent earthquakes.

mcqs.q: The point on Earth's surface directly above the hypocentre/focus of an earthquake is called the:
mcqs.explanation: The epicentre (or epicentrum) is the point on Earth's surface directly above the hypocentre/focus of an earthquake.

mcqs.q: Surface waves are typically generated when the source of an earthquake is:
mcqs.explanation: Surface waves are typically generated when the source of the earthquake is close to the Earth's surface.

mcqs.q: A P-wave (compressional wave) is a seismic body wave that shakes the ground:
options: Back and forth in the same direction as the wave is moving
mcqs.explanation: A P-wave or compressional wave shakes the ground back and forth in the same direction (and the opposite direction) as the wave is moving.

mcqs.q: What was the magnitude of the largest earthquake ever recorded (1960, Chile)?
mcqs.explanation: The 1960 Valdivian (Great Chilean) earthquake had a magnitude of 9.5 — the largest ever recorded.

mcqs.q: A volcanic feature formed by the collapse of a volcano into itself is called a:
mcqs.explanation: A caldera is a volcanic feature formed by the collapse of a volcano into itself — typically after a major eruption empties the magma chamber.

mcqs.q: According to the US Geologic Survey, approximately how many potentially active volcanoes are there worldwide?
mcqs.explanation: According to the United States Geologic Survey, there are approximately 1,350 potentially active volcanoes worldwide.

mcqs.q: The material that comes out of a volcano during eruption is in which states?
mcqs.explanation: The material that comes out of a volcano at the time of eruption is solid (ash, rocks), liquid (lava) and gaseous (volcanic gases).

mcqs.q: Iceland has approximately how many volcanoes (many still active)?
mcqs.explanation: Iceland has over 200 volcanoes — many of which are still active.

mcqs.q: Mount Vesuvius in Italy is currently classified as:
mcqs.explanation: Vesuvius in Italy is a dormant volcano at present — it has not erupted since 1944.

mcqs.q: Mauna Loa is an example of which type of volcano?
mcqs.explanation: Mauna Loa (Hawaii) is an example of an active shield volcano — characterised by broad, gentle slopes built by low-viscosity lava.

mcqs.q: When magma is ejected by a volcano, the material is called:
mcqs.explanation: When magma is ejected by a volcano or other vent, the material is called lava.

mcqs.q: Which is the MOST COMMON type of volcano?
mcqs.explanation: Scoria cones, also known as cinder cones, are the most common type of volcano.

mcqs.q: Cinder cones are also known as:
mcqs.explanation: Cinder cones are also known as scoria cones.

mcqs.q: Stratovolcanoes are also called:
mcqs.explanation: Stratovolcanoes are also called composite volcanoes — built up of alternating layers of lava flows and pyroclastic material.

mcqs.q: How many main types of volcanoes are there?
mcqs.explanation: There are four types of volcanoes: shield volcanoes, lava domes, cinder cones (scoria cones) and composite volcanoes (stratovolcanoes).

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the four main types of volcanoes?
mcqs.explanation: There is no 'magma volcano' type. The four types are shield, lava dome, cinder cone and composite volcanoes.

mcqs.q: Mt. Semeru — which erupted lava on December 4, 2021 killing 14 people — is located in:
mcqs.explanation: Mt. Semeru on Java Island, Indonesia erupted lava on December 4, 2021, killing 14 people.

mcqs.q: Which type of volcano has broad, gentle slopes formed by low-viscosity lava?
mcqs.explanation: Shield volcanoes have broad, gentle slopes formed by low-viscosity lava that flows great distances. Mauna Loa is the classic example.

mcqs.q: From which language does the word 'tsunami' originate?
mcqs.explanation: Tsunami is a word of Japanese language — meaning 'harbour wave'.

mcqs.q: A tsunami is an ocean wave triggered by:
options: Large earthquakes near or under the ocean, volcanic eruptions, submarine landslides, or onshore landslides into water
mcqs.explanation: A tsunami is an ocean wave triggered by large earthquakes near or under the ocean, volcanic eruptions, submarine landslides or onshore landslides where large volumes of debris fall into the water.

mcqs.q: What percentage of all tsunamis happen within the Pacific Ring of Fire?
mcqs.explanation: About 80% of tsunamis happen within the Pacific Ocean's Ring of Fire.

mcqs.q: Tsunamis are most commonly triggered by:
mcqs.explanation: Tsunamis are most commonly triggered by large earthquakes that occur near or under the ocean.

mcqs.q: A flood occurs when:
options: Water inundates land that is normally dry
mcqs.explanation: A flood occurs when water inundates land that's normally dry — through a multitude of ways (heavy rain, dam breaks, storm surges).

mcqs.q: An avalanche is best described as:
options: Masses of snow, ice and rocks falling rapidly down a mountainside
mcqs.explanation: An avalanche is masses of snow, ice and rocks that fall rapidly down a mountainside.

mcqs.q: A cyclone is a system of winds rotating:
options: Clockwise around a high-pressure centre (Northern Hemisphere)
Counterclockwise around a low-pressure centre (Northern Hemisphere)
mcqs.explanation: A cyclone is a system of winds rotating counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere around a low-pressure centre.

mcqs.q: Tropical cyclones of the Bay of Bengal are usually called:
mcqs.explanation: The tropical cyclones of the Bay of Bengal are usually called Hurricanes.

mcqs.q: An anticyclone is caused by:
options: Winds rotating around a low-pressure centre
Winds blowing spirally outwards from the centre
mcqs.explanation: An anticyclone is caused by winds that blow spirally outwards from the centre.

mcqs.q: Hurricanes are tropical storms that form over the:
options: North Atlantic Ocean and Northeast Pacific
mcqs.explanation: Hurricanes are tropical storms that form over the North Atlantic Ocean and Northeast Pacific.

mcqs.q: Typhoons are formed over which ocean?
mcqs.explanation: Typhoons are formed over the Northwest Pacific Ocean — they are the same phenomenon as hurricanes but in a different basin.

mcqs.q: A drought is best defined as:
options: A period of below-normal precipitation in an area or region
mcqs.explanation: A drought is a period of time when an area or region experiences below-normal precipitation.

mcqs.q: A wildfire is:
options: An unplanned fire that burns in a natural area such as a forest, grassland or prairie
mcqs.explanation: A wildfire is an unplanned fire that burns in a natural area such as a forest, grassland or prairie.

mcqs.q: A landslide is the:
options: Downward movement of rocks and debris that detach from the underlying slope
mcqs.explanation: A landslide is the downward movement of rocks and debris that detach from the underlying slope.

mcqs.q: A blizzard is a severe snowstorm characterised by sustained winds of at least:
mcqs.explanation: A blizzard is a severe snowstorm characterised by strong sustained winds of at least 56 km/h, lasting for a prolonged period of three hours or more.

mcqs.q: Electricity is what kind of source of energy?
mcqs.explanation: Electricity is a secondary source of energy — it is generated from primary sources like coal, gas, sun, wind or water.

mcqs.q: Renewable energy is best defined as energy:
options: Derived from natural processes replenished at a rate equal to or faster than consumption
mcqs.explanation: Renewable energy is energy derived from natural processes that are replenished at a rate equal to or faster than the rate at which they are consumed.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is NOT a renewable energy source?
mcqs.explanation: Coal is a NON-renewable fossil fuel. Solar, wind, geothermal, hydro, tidal and biomass are renewable.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is a non-renewable energy source?
mcqs.explanation: Natural gas (and other fossil fuels — coal, oil) is a non-renewable energy source.

mcqs.q: Water stored in a dam possesses what type of energy?
mcqs.explanation: Water stored in a dam possesses potential energy — which is converted to kinetic energy when released through turbines.

mcqs.q: A gamma ray is best described as:
options: A packet of electromagnetic energy (photon) emitted by the nucleus during radioactive decay
mcqs.explanation: A gamma ray is a packet of electromagnetic energy (photon) emitted by the nucleus of some radionuclides following radioactive decay.

mcqs.q: All forms of energy that we use are ultimately transformed into:
mcqs.explanation: All forms of energy that we use are ultimately transformed into heat energy (per the laws of thermodynamics).

mcqs.q: Chemical energy is stored in:
mcqs.explanation: Chemical energy is stored in a dry cell (battery) — and released as electrical energy when the cell is connected in a circuit.

mcqs.q: Which element is required for solar energy conversion?
mcqs.explanation: Silicon is the element required for solar energy conversion — it is the primary material in photovoltaic cells.

mcqs.q: Hydropower energy is harnessed from:
options: Water stored in dams or flowing in rivers
mcqs.explanation: Hydropower (hydro energy) uses water stored in dams as well as flowing in rivers to create electricity in hydropower plants.

mcqs.q: Wind energy describes the process by which:
options: Wind is used to generate electricity
mcqs.explanation: Wind energy (wind power) describes the process by which wind is used to generate electricity — typically via wind turbines.

mcqs.q: Biomass energy comes from:
options: Any organic, decomposing matter derived from plants or animals available on a renewable basis
mcqs.explanation: Biomass energy comes from any organic — i.e. decomposing — matter derived from plants or animals available on a renewable basis.
